data.title: CubeSmart signals continued improvement in operating fundamentals through 2025 with revised guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CubeSmart (CUBE) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Christopher P. Marr reported, "We posted a very solid beat and raise second quarter. The stabilization trends we experienced in the first quarter continued their positive momentum throughout the second quarter and into the month of July." Marr noted that key performance indicators have exceeded expectations set at the beginning of the year, citing a 190 basis point growth in trough-to-peak occupancy and a 28.3% increase in net effective rates for new customers compared to 15% in 2024.
  • Marr highlighted urban markets along the Acela Corridor and Chicago as top performers, while Sunbelt markets, especially Florida and Arizona, continued to lag due to new supply absorption. The New York MSA was described as a standout, with the boroughs leading performance and Long Island and Northern New Jersey showing improvement as supply is absorbed.
  • Marr stated, "Looking ahead, we expect the baseline for occupancy and move-in rates should approach parity by the end of the year," while acknowledging ongoing risks from governmental and monetary policy impacts.
  • CFO Timothy M. Martin said, "Same-store revenue growth was down 0.5% over last year, with average occupancy for our same-store portfolio down 80 basis points to 90.6% during the quarter." He added, "Same-store operating expenses grew 1.2% over last year, again, this quarter, trending a bit better than our expectations."
  • Martin explained that the company reported FFO per share as adjusted of $0.65 for the quarter, at the high end of the guidance range. He also noted, "We added 30 stores to our third-party management platform during the quarter, bringing that total to 873 stores at quarter end."

Outlook

  • Management raised the midpoint of revenue guidance while narrowing the top end, citing that the highest demand scenario did not materialize during the busy season. Martin stated, "The top end would have assumed a stronger improvement in overall levels of demand. And we're just reining in the high end of those expectations as a result of that's not how the busy season played out."
  • Martin indicated that same-store revenue growth is expected to be slightly more negative in the third quarter compared to the second, with improvement anticipated in the fourth quarter.
  • Marr expressed confidence, saying, "We believe we're in a good position here as we navigate the back half of '25, and we're optimistic about what 2026 will hold for us."

Financial Results

  • Martin reported that revenue growth of negative 0.5% combined with 1.2% expense growth led to negative 1.1% same-store NOI growth for the quarter.
  • FFO per share as adjusted was $0.65, which Martin said was "at the high end of our guidance range entering the quarter."
  • Martin noted strong balance sheet metrics, with net debt-to-EBITDA at 4.7x and a $300 million senior unsecured note maturing in November 2025. The company is focused on issuing long-term unsecured debt to extend its maturity schedule.
  • The third-party management platform added 30 stores, totaling 873 at quarter end, despite some churn from portfolio sales.
  • Favorable insurance renewal, successful property tax appeals, and efficiency initiatives contributed to improved expense control.

Q&A

  • Samir Upadhyay Khanal, BofA Securities: Asked about the rationale for raising the guidance midpoint but lowering the top end. Martin answered, "The top end would have assumed a stronger improvement in overall levels of demand. And we're just reining in the high end of those expectations." Khanal also inquired about New York revenue growth deceleration. Marr responded that net rental income in the boroughs and Long Island accelerated, while Northern New Jersey is gradually improving as supply is absorbed.
  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS: Wanted clarification on customer turnover and recovery pace. Martin described the recovery as gradual, emphasizing, "The most encouraging thing about 2025 is it's feeling more normal... certainly more normal than it has been over the last 2 years." Goldsmith also asked about acquisition activity. Martin said, "Not a lot has changed from what we've talked about in the prior handful of quarters... just not able to transact in any meaningful way."
  • Todd Michael Thomas, KeyBanc: Asked if Sunbelt markets would take longer to recover. Marr replied, "I do think it will be a longer time frame for those higher impacted supply markets to recover, but I do think trends will get better."
  • Nicholas Gregory Joseph, Citi: Asked about construction starts and supply. Marr said, "I think it's actually the opposite. For the most part, as you would expect, raw material costs are up, land values certainly have not gone down... trying to pencil out returns that make sense in this market today... is also a challenge."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on clarifications around guidance, recovery pace, and market-level performance. Questions sought explanation of narrowed guidance ranges and the gradual nature of recovery, without overt skepticism.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, using phrases such as "we believe we're in a good position" and consistently referencing stabilization and positive trends. During Q&A, management was transparent about challenges and cautious with expectations, especially regarding revenue growth and supply absorption.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management sentiment shifted from cautious optimism to a more constructive and confident outlook, with management more willing to discuss improvement and stabilization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured a raised guidance midpoint, whereas the previous quarter maintained guidance due to macro uncertainty despite a strong start.
  • Management tone evolved from measured optimism to more explicit confidence, citing stabilization trends and narrowing negative gaps in occupancy and rates.
  • Analysts in the current quarter focused more on guidance range details, recovery timing, and market-specific supply/demand dynamics, as opposed to the previous quarter's broader questions about demand drivers and expense controls.
  • Key metrics, such as occupancy and FFO per share, showed incremental improvement against expectations, with expense control remaining a highlight.
  • Strategic focus shifted towards managing supply headwinds in Sunbelt markets and optimizing the third-party management platform amid ongoing portfolio churn.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged continued risk from consumer volatility driven by governmental and monetary policy changes.
  • Supply headwinds remain a concern, particularly in Sunbelt markets like Florida, Arizona, Atlanta, and Houston, where new deliveries are still being absorbed.
  • Some churn in the third-party management platform was attributed to increased portfolio transactions, with management indicating limited visibility into future churn but ongoing onboarding efforts.
  • Expense increases in certain markets, such as Austin, were explained as due to property tax timing and difficult comps from prior-year refunds.

Final Takeaway

Management reported a strong second quarter with continued stabilization and improvement in key operating metrics, leading to a raised guidance midpoint for the year. Urban markets, especially the New York MSA and Chicago, outperformed, while Sunbelt regions continue to face supply pressures but are showing constructive trends. Expense controls, favorable insurance renewals, and ongoing efficiency initiatives contributed to positive results, and management expressed confidence in the company's positioning for the remainder of 2025 and into 2026, despite ongoing supply and macroeconomic risks.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CNH reaffirms 2025 EPS guidance of $0.50 to $0.70 while advancing Starlink partnership and inventory reductions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CNH Industrial N.V. (CNH) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Gerrit Andreas Marx stated that "market conditions in the quarter remained soft" and described the quarter as "financially muted by design while reducing our channel inventories." He emphasized the deliberate strategy to "keep production very low to help reduce CNH dealer inventories and clear aged inventories while defending market share." Management highlighted that industrial production hours were down 12% year-over-year, with agriculture and construction both down double digits. Marx also announced a new collaboration with Starlink, which enables "superfast and cost-efficient connectivity in all remote areas for our farmers." He affirmed the company's focus on clearing used inventories and preparing for the model year 2026 machines.
  • CFO James A. J. Nickolas reported that "second quarter industrial net sales were down 16% year-over-year to $4 billion," mainly due to lower shipment volumes and reduced dealer inventory requirements. He stated, "Q2 free cash flow from Industrial Activities was $451 million, significantly better compared to Q2 of 2024 as improvements in working capital more than offset the lower industrial EBIT." Nickolas also noted, "Second quarter gross margin was 21.8%, down from the 24.4% in Q2 2024, affected by the lower production volumes and the very unfavorable geographic mix."

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed full-year guidance, with Marx stating, "We are reaffirming our net sales and EBIT margin guidance." He added, "Full-year pricing is expected to be positive despite the need for additional targeted incentives to help continue the dealers' new and used unit de-stocking efforts." Nickolas indicated, "We are also reaffirming our EPS guidance at the prior range of $0.50 to $0.70." Management signaled that global industry forecast for agriculture remains "down around 10% from 2024," and 2025 is expected to be a "trough level of global market demand." The company expects Q3 production slots are full and Q4 slots are half full, with some North American products nearly full for Q4.

Financial Results

  • CNH reported consolidated revenues for the quarter of $4.7 billion, a 14% decrease. The Agriculture segment saw sales of $3.2 billion, down 17% year-over-year, including a 36% decline in North America. Industrial adjusted EBIT was $224 million, a 55% decrease. EPS for the quarter was $0.17. Nickolas stated, "Adjusted net income decreased by about half with adjusted diluted earnings per share down from $0.35 to $0.17." Free cash flow from Industrial Activities was $451 million. Gross margin for the quarter was 21.8%. Construction net sales were $773 million, down 13%. Financial Services reported net income of $87 million, with a noted increase in risk costs in Brazil. Retail originations were $2.7 billion. The managed portfolio ended the quarter at nearly $29 billion.

Q&A

  • Angel Castillo, Morgan Stanley: Asked for details on inventory reduction and confidence in achieving retail sales-type production by year-end. Marx responded that CNH has "reduced another $200 million from that level" of excess inventory and is "pretty well on track with what we said at the beginning of the year," noting particular attention to small and medium tractors in North America due to import supply chains and tariffs.
  • Kyle David Menges, Citigroup: Inquired about order book trends for 2026. Marx indicated, "the trough to be 2025," and discussed the need for clarity on tariffs and global trade deals for better forecasting, mentioning early positive signs in EMEA and readiness in South America.
  • Jamie Lyn Cook, Truist Securities: Sought clarification on levers for earnings growth in 2026 and pricing strategy. Marx explained, "pricing will be positive for the full year" and highlighted cost discipline, sourcing, and quality improvements as key factors. He detailed that price increases for model year '26 are partly to offset tariffs.
  • Timothy W. Thein, Raymond James: Asked about the alignment of production and retail figures. Marx clarified that "this is units, not value, and it's very different by region," explaining underproduction in high-value segments and the importance of mix.
  • Kristen Owen, Oppenheimer: Queried about European demand trends. Marx noted that "demand trend to be playing out predominantly in tractors" and shared that CNH was "defending and here and there even slightly growing our shares in tractors in some countries."
  • David Michael Raso, Evercore ISI: Asked about price/cost expectations and tariff impacts. Nickolas responded, "we do expect it to be positive for the full year...it's tracking what we thought it would be." Marx added that positive pricing will continue for the remainder of the year with cost improvements, although the FIFO benefit on tariffs is not expected to repeat in Q3 and Q4.
  • Tami Zakaria, JPMorgan: Asked about implied Ag segment operating margins for Q3 and Q4. Nickolas answered, "Q3 to have a little bit of a step down versus Q2 as it's typical. And then, of course, a strong rebound in Q4."
  • Michael Shlisky, D.A. Davidson: Sought insights from the new CFO on priorities. Nickolas confirmed that "quality is a primary focus," with lower quality expenses year-to-date and ongoing benefits from strategic sourcing.
  • Edward Randolph Jackson, Northland Securities: Asked about North American inventory alignment and incentives. Marx acknowledged the "price differential between used and new" and described increased financial efforts with dealers as "in the numbers" for guidance.
  • Avinatan Jaroslawicz, UBS: Asked about timing of tariff impacts. Nickolas projected "almost all of it, close to $120 million is our negative effect on EBIT in the second half of this year," with ongoing mitigation efforts.
  • Daniela Costa, Goldman Sachs: Queried on delinquencies and construction partnership strategy. Nickolas attributed the rise in delinquencies to Brazil, stating, "the issue is largely isolated to Brazil," while Marx clarified that construction partnership discussions are ongoing but paced due to tariff uncertainties.
  • David Michael Raso, Evercore ISI: Requested margin breakdown by region. Nickolas said, "North America is our highest margin region. EMEA is a -- comes in second...LATAM coming in third."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was inquisitive, often pressing for clarity on inventory reduction, order trends, tariff impacts, and regional performance, reflecting a slightly negative to neutral sentiment due to ongoing market headwinds and uncertainty.
  • Management maintained a disciplined and pragmatic tone, focusing on operational execution, cost control, inventory management, and strategic initiatives. Confidence was evident in statements like "We are reaffirming our net sales and EBIT margin guidance" and "We are in this for the long term," but caution was shown regarding tariffs and macro uncertainty.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remains consistently focused on long-term positioning and operational discipline, while analysts' sentiment has shifted from uncertainty about the macro environment to a sharper focus on inventory progress and tariff mitigation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • CNH continued intentional production cuts and inventory reductions, with a further $200 million reduction in Ag inventory during Q2, building on the $100 million reduction in Q1.
  • The company reaffirmed its EPS guidance of $0.50 to $0.70 for the year, consistent with the prior quarter.
  • Pricing actions in Q2 and for model year '26 show a more proactive response to anticipated tariff costs than in Q1, with management now emphasizing the offsetting of tariff impacts through a combination of pricing, cost savings, and supplier negotiations.
  • The transition to new CFO Jim Nickolas is complete, with a renewed focus on quality and cost structure highlighted in Q2.
  • Analysts' focus remained on inventory normalization, order visibility for 2026, tariff impacts, and regional performance, with more specific questions about margin breakdown by region and timing of tariff effects.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing "complexity and uncertainty in the macroeconomic environment," specifically mentioning soft commodity prices, high commodity stock levels, and uncertain end markets for U.S. farm production.
  • Tariffs and the potential for retaliatory actions remain a significant risk, with Marx noting, "We do not, however, expect that this will necessarily continue in Q3 and Q4 as it depends on how and when the U.S. tariffs and potential retaliations will impact our industry."
  • Delinquencies in Financial Services have increased, "almost exclusively attributable to activity in Brazil," with reserves increased accordingly.
  • The company is monitoring foreign exchange impacts, with Nickolas noting that "the overall negative translation effect on net sales is coming from other areas: Brazil, Australia and Canada."
  • Construction partnership decisions are paused or slowed due to tariff uncertainty and market conditions.

Final Takeaway

CNH Industrial is navigating a trough year for global agriculture demand in 2025 with a disciplined approach to production, aggressive inventory reductions, and targeted pricing actions to offset tariff and cost pressures. Management reaffirmed guidance for net sales, EBIT margin, and EPS, while highlighting new technology initiatives like the Starlink partnership and ongoing operational improvements. Despite persistent macro and trade-related risks, the company remains focused on achieving normalized dealer inventory levels by year-end and is positioning itself for recovery and growth in 2026, with operational efficiency, quality, and innovation as key priorities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Dorian LPG signals strong Q2 TCE rates above $67,000/day while advancing fleet upgrades
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dorian LPG Ltd. (LPG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • John C. Hadjipateras, CEO, highlighted a dividend of $0.60 per share, totaling $25.6 million, stating it "reflects our commitment to returning capital to shareholders in a manner that's aligned with market conditions and our policy of distributing earnings prudently." He noted this is the 16th dividend payment, bringing total dividends distributed to over $665 million and total capital returned to shareholders above $900 million.
  • Hadjipateras reported market resilience in Q2, with strengthened freight rates supported by "healthy arbitrage economics and geopolitical tensions in the Middle East." He emphasized the impact of tariff escalation and shifting trade routes, as well as ongoing growth in U.S. LPG exports and increased Middle Eastern exports.
  • Operationally, the company completed 10 of 12 planned dry dockings for 2025 and advanced the conversion of some VLGCs to facilitate ammonia carriage.
  • CFO Theodore B. Young stated, "We reported a TCE per available day of $39,726, which was a good result despite our heavy dry dock schedule during the quarter that resulted in some 195 days that were not available for revenue generation." Young added, "Our June results were much stronger than the previous 2 months, which is indicative of the stronger market environment."
  • Young disclosed, "We currently estimate that we have fixed approximately 70% of the pool's fixable days in the quarter at a TCE in excess of $67,000 per day."
  • Young provided further detail: "At June 30, 2025, we reported $278 million of free cash. Cash flow during the quarter was affected by our drydocking cash outlays and the foregone revenue, but we still finished with a very healthy cash balance."
  • Taro Rasmussen commented on market fundamentals, referencing "a steady rise in freight markets" and the adaptability of the VLGC industry amid geopolitical shocks and tariff impacts.
  • John C. Lycouris noted the conversion of VLGCs to ammonia carriers, stating, "Once this last vessel is completed, 5 VLGC vessels in our Dorian VLGC LPG fleet will be able to carry ammonia cargoes, which includes our new building VLGC-VLAC vessel, which delivers in 2026."

Outlook

  • Management expects forward bookings for the quarter ending September 30, 2025, to reflect "a strong increase in rates since late May into June," with approximately 70% of fixable days at a TCE in excess of $67,000 per day.
  • The company anticipates the quarterly time charter-in expense will be approximately $14 million to $15 million going forward.
  • Young said, "We expect our cash cost per day for the coming year to be approximately $26,000 per day, excluding the remaining capital expenditures for dry docking and the progress payments on our new building."

Financial Results

  • The company reported a TCE per available day of $39,726.
  • Daily OpEx for the quarter was $10,108 excluding drydocking expenses, down from the prior quarter's $11,001.
  • Noncapitalized drydocking expenses totaled about $2.6 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $38.6 million, with an adjusted figure of $49.5 million excluding bonuses and drydocking expenses.
  • Free cash at quarter-end was $278 million, and the debt balance was $543.5 million.
  • Debt to total book capitalization stood at 34.4%, and net debt to total cap at 16.8%.
  • Dividend declared was $0.60 per share, or about $25.6 million in total.

Q&A

  • Omar Mostafa Nokta, Jefferies: "Could you maybe just give a sense of what's driving this market? What has really propelled it from where it was earlier this year?" Taro Rasmussen responded that market fundamentals, especially strong U.S. NGL production and the ability to export, are primary drivers. He noted, "It's a very healthy reflection of the wider market that using experiences from the past whenever there's been trade barriers between the U.S. and China that industry players were able to reflect on past learnings, adapt them and get trade going back to normal as soon as possible."
  • Nokta asked about changes in the U.S. export arbitrage and freight rate capture. Rasmussen cited last year's anomalies and this year's external geopolitical factors, adding, "This year has been more driven by other external factors in other parts of the world and political in nature and geopolitics."
  • On the potential of ethane carriers entering the VLGC market, Hadjipateras stated, "If the ethane trade for some reason, were stopped, they would be entering the VLGC market."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed curiosity and a cautiously optimistic tone, probing the sustainability and drivers of market strength, notably inquiring about trade flows and freight rate dynamics.
  • Management maintained a confident and detailed tone, emphasizing market adaptability and strong fundamentals. Young described the dividend as reflecting "a constructive market view."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone shifted from concern over volatility to a more constructive, fact-finding approach. Management’s confidence increased, with more discussion of concrete forward rates and operational achievements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Dividend increased from $0.50 per share in Q4 2025 to $0.60 per share in Q1 2026.
  • TCE per available day rose from $35,300 in Q4 2025 to $39,726 in Q1 2026.
  • Free cash decreased from $317 million at March 31, 2025, to $278 million at June 30, 2025, reflecting drydocking and capital outlays.
  • Management’s tone shifted from caution due to market volatility and tariffs in Q4 to a more positive outlook in Q1, highlighting increasing rates and completed fleet upgrades.
  • Analysts' questions evolved from concerns about volatility and trade pattern shifts to probing the sustainability and underlying drivers of current market strength.
  • Strategic focus expanded from managing tariff impacts and drydockings to leveraging increased market rates and advancing ammonia cargo capability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and the volatility caused by global tariff announcements as key risks.
  • There is ongoing uncertainty in global trade policy and potential for future disruption from new or escalated tariffs.
  • Operational risks include the impact of drydocking schedules on available revenue days.
  • Management highlighted mitigation strategies, including the completion of most drydockings, conversion of vessels for ammonia carriage, and ongoing monitoring of vessel performance to minimize future operational interruptions.

Final Takeaway

Dorian LPG closed its first quarter of 2026 with a stronger market backdrop, increased dividend, and improved vessel utilization despite ongoing geopolitical and trade-related volatility. Management underscored the company’s adaptability, prudent capital allocation, and commitment to shareholder returns, while signaling confidence in maintaining strong rates and operational momentum for the remainder of the year, supported by completed fleet upgrades and a constructive rate environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Gray Media to buy Block's TV stations for $80M
data.description:
Gray Media (NYSE:GTN) has reached an agreement to buy Block Communications' television stations for $80M, the company said in a statement on Friday.

The acquisition will strengthen Gray's presence in the Midwest market by creating a new "Big Four" duopoly in one market and by adding top-ranked local news stations in two more midwestern markets.

The transaction includes WDRB and WBKI, the FOX and CW affiliates for the Louisville, Kentucky, market (DMA 49), where Gray owns and operates NBC affiliate WAVE3.

The transaction also includes WAND, the NBC affiliate for the Springfield-Champaign-Decatur, Illinois, market (DMA 92), and WLIO, the NBC affiliate for the Lima, Ohio, market (DMA 190), as well as WLIO’s associated low-power television stations.

The acquisition is expected to be completed in the fourth quarter of this year.

Source: Press Release

data.title: Huntsman signals continued balance sheet discipline and cost focus amid tariff and market volatility
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Huntsman Corporation (HUN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Peter R. Huntsman reported that "our second quarter results were not unexpected and came in about where we thought they would." He highlighted a rebound in Advanced Materials earnings, though this was "offsetting the disappointing sluggishness of construction activity and tariff uncertainty, especially in polyurethanes."
  • Huntsman emphasized that "volatility caused by tariff and trade disputes over the past few months is starting to dissipate at least as of 12 hours ago," but noted that "inventories remain very low in most of our downstream supply chain, while consumer confidence seems to be muted."
  • He described the company's approach as neither panicked nor overly optimistic for the third quarter, but stated, "long term, we do anticipate an improvement in construction and perhaps some gradual change as China seems to be focusing more on their overcapacity."
  • Huntsman outlined a continued focus on balance sheet discipline: "we will continue to be extremely prudent on spending capital beyond our normalized run rate of safety, maintenance and reliability," and reiterated ongoing cost reduction and cash generation efforts.
  • He explained that aggressive inventory and working capital management allowed Huntsman to generate positive cash flow in Q2, though this "cost us about $25 million of EBITDA in the second quarter." He added that this impact would be less pronounced in Q3.
  • The company continues reviewing its asset portfolio and engaging with shareholders, with Huntsman stating, "the last thing we want to be doing is sitting around waiting for things to get better."
  • On strategic outlook, he mentioned usual seasonality ahead, possible influences from higher tariffs and duties for MDI in North America, a possible interest rate cut, cost reduction benefits, and an emphasis on "prices over volume."
  • CFO Philip M. Lister noted, "No, we're not looking at any new debt issuance in the second half of the year. I think you're probably aware of where we sit from a maturity standpoint, which is our revolver matures in May of 2027. We've got our securitization facilities as well. And then our longer-term bond maturities are '29, '31, '34, which is helpful, frankly, in the current trough environment overall."

Outlook

  • Management anticipates neither major improvement nor significant deterioration in the near term, with Huntsman saying, "we see neither reason to panic nor to be overly optimistic" for the third quarter.
  • Huntsman stated, "Over the next few quarters, we will see usual seasonality, but also the possible influences of higher tariffs and duties for MDI coming into North American markets, a possible interest rate cut, the benefits of more of our cost reductions falling to the bottom line and hopefully, a greater focus on prices over volume."
  • The company expects the impact of aggressive inventory management to diminish in Q3, as noted by Huntsman.

Financial Results

  • Huntsman reported, "Our aggressive inventory and working capital focus allowed us to generate positive cash flow in the second quarter. This cost us about $25 million of EBITDA in the second quarter. This charge was offset by reduced bonus accruals and other smaller onetime benefits."
  • Lister added, "We did bring in $40 million from our Chinese joint venture liquidation. So theoretically, over the last 12 months, we covered the $170 million of dividend."
  • The company recognized a $7 million benefit in Performance Products from a reversal of a loss contingency accrual related to U.S. operations.

Q&A

  • Kevin William McCarthy, Vertical Research Partners, asked about MDI utilization rates. Huntsman replied, "the industry is operating somewhere in the low to mid-80 percentile. That's going to probably be a little bit higher than that in North America, perhaps a little bit lower than that in China."
  • Patrick David Cunningham, Citi, asked about order book trends and customer sentiment. Huntsman responded, "stable would probably be the best singular description of what we're seeing across the board... inventories are probably lower than usual... people are kind of ordering just what they need for the next 30 days or so."
  • Cunningham also asked about China supply rationalization. Huntsman explained, "China, where you have the greatest concentration of production continues to be our most profitable market for MDI, and our business in China is performing quite well in comparison to North America and very well in comparison to Europe."
  • Frank Joseph Mitsch, Fermium Research, asked about the dividend. Huntsman replied, "we feel that we're in a pretty good place right now" and emphasized ongoing review by the Board.
  • Vincent Stephen Andrews, Morgan Stanley, inquired about trade finality. Huntsman said, "Our biggest issue is volatility... If we're going to pay higher tariffs, if they're going to be duties, if they're going to be barriers, whatever, let's figure out what they are... But it's just... it's very volatile."
  • Other questions covered Advanced Materials focus for bolt-on acquisitions, maleic anhydride facility closure, European footprint, and the impact of tariffs on new MDI plant investment.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious sentiment, focusing on utilization, order stability, tariffs, pricing, and capital allocation, with a generally neutral to slightly negative tone on near-term recovery prospects.
  • Management maintained a measured and disciplined tone, focusing on cost control and balance sheet management, with Huntsman stating, "we see neither reason to panic nor to be overly optimistic."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from discussing short-term supply chain disruptions to a more stable but cautious outlook, with ongoing emphasis on cost and cash discipline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language in Q2 was more measured, with less discussion of imminent recovery and greater emphasis on stability and prudence.
  • Strategic focus remained on cost structure, balance sheet management, and cash generation, with additional attention to asset portfolio review and shareholder engagement in Q2.
  • Analysts continued to probe on utilization, order trends, tariffs, and capital allocation, but with slightly less urgency compared to Q1, reflecting a stabilization of volatility.
  • Key metric changes included explicit mention of positive cash flow generation in Q2 and a $25 million EBITDA impact from inventory management, with ongoing focus on dividend coverage and debt maturities.
  • Management's confidence in cost discipline remained firm, while expressing limited visibility for significant near-term improvement, consistent with the prior quarter's cautious stance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Persistent macroeconomic volatility, particularly relating to tariffs and trade disputes, was highlighted by management.
  • Ongoing sluggishness in construction activity and muted consumer confidence continue to impact demand, especially in polyurethanes.
  • Competitive pricing pressures in Europe, particularly in polyurethanes, were noted as a challenge.
  • The company faces potential impacts from higher tariffs and duties for MDI in North America and uncertain timing for recovery in construction markets.
  • Management is actively reviewing asset portfolio, focusing on cost reductions, and maintaining working capital discipline to mitigate these risks.

Final Takeaway

Huntsman Corporation management emphasized disciplined balance sheet management, ongoing cost reduction efforts, and a prudent approach to capital allocation as the company navigates ongoing macroeconomic and trade-related volatility. The second quarter saw a rebound in Advanced Materials but continued sluggishness in construction and polyurethanes, with positive cash flow achieved through aggressive inventory management despite a related EBITDA impact. While order patterns remain stable and inventory levels are low, management expects only gradual improvement in end markets and will prioritize value creation through cost control and strategic asset review in the quarters ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Marcus Corporation signals capital expenditure slowdown for 2026 as renovation cycle concludes
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Marcus Corporation (MCS) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Chad M. Paris, CFO, opened the call by highlighting, "Consolidated revenues of $206 million were up 17% compared to the prior year quarter, with revenue before cost reimbursements growing in both divisions." He also stated, "Operating income for the quarter was $13 million, an increase of $10.8 million compared to the prior year quarter." Paris emphasized that theater division revenues increased nearly 30% year over year and cited a 26.7% jump in comparable theater attendance.
  • Paris detailed, "Consolidated adjusted EBITDA for the second quarter was $32.3 million, a nearly 47% increase over the second quarter of fiscal 2024. Net earnings for the quarter was $7.3 million or $0.23 per share compared to a net loss of $5.2 million or $0.17 per share, excluding the impacts of our convertible debt repurchases in the second quarter last year."
  • Paris noted, "Our average admission price increased 2% during the second quarter of fiscal 2025 compared to last year, which was impacted by a favorable mix of films that attracted audiences to PLF screens and by headwinds from our strategies to drive attendance..."
  • CEO Gregory S. Marcus remarked, "Today, we are thrilled to report second quarter results with significant growth that was driven by a high-quality and diverse film slate, coupled with strong consumer demand to experience these blockbuster films in the big screen."
  • Marcus highlighted the momentum, stating, "Overall, we are pleased with the results for the quarter and we entered the third quarter with solid momentum."

Outlook

  • Paris stated, "We continue to expect capital expenditures for fiscal 2025 of $70 million to $85 million. The timing of several projects will impact our final capital expenditure number for the year, and we will update our estimates as the year progresses."
  • Marcus discussed future strategy: "As we start to think about 2026, we do see a meaningful step down in capital expenditures as we get past the heavy part of the reinvestment cycle, and that we are in this year with our current hotel portfolio."
  • Marcus expressed confidence in upcoming film slates and hotel bookings, detailing, "Group room pays for 2026 continues to run 20% ahead of where we were at this time last year for the next year out, with banquet and catering revenues similarly running ahead of last year's pace."

Financial Results

  • Paris reported, "Theater division adjusted EBITDA during the second quarter of fiscal '25 was $26.5 million, a 76% increase over the prior year quarter."
  • In the hotel segment, Paris said, "Total revenues before cost reimbursements were $64.6 million for the second quarter of fiscal 2025, a 1.2% increase compared to the prior year. The RevPAR for our comparable owned hotels decreased 2.9% during the second quarter compared to the prior year, which resulted from an overall occupancy rate decrease of 5.4 percentage points, partially offset by a 5% increase in our average daily rate, or ADR."
  • Paris noted, "Cash flow from operations was $31.6 million in the second quarter of fiscal 2025 compared to cash flow from operations of $36 million in the prior year quarter... Total capital expenditures during the second quarter of fiscal '25 were $16.9 million compared to $19.8 million in the second quarter of fiscal '24."

Q&A

  • Eric Wold, Texas Capital Securities: Asked about the geographic split in group pace for hotels and details on blockbuster pricing surcharges in theaters. Paris responded, "Our group pace gains have in part been because we've got renovated meeting space... I don't have specific splits between how much is Milwaukee versus the rest of our portfolio."
  • Wold also inquired about the size of blockbuster surcharges. Paris replied, "That's moving that program from $7 to $7.50 and on certain films $8.50. And that $1 delta on certain films is what we're doing with generally with blockbuster pricing across a number of different films."
  • Andrew Edward Crum, B. Riley Securities: Questioned domestic box office outlook for the second half. Marcus stated, "We -- I find it very hard to predict how the box office is going to do in any period of -- any short period of time. It just it's -- you just don't know. But it looks very positive, and we should end up strong."
  • Crum also asked about puts and takes for Q3 hotel revenue. Paris responded, "We've done extremely well with our banquet and catering business ... It does come at a bit lower margins... the impact of the Hilton renovation... when you strip that out, I think all things considered, we're pretty happy with the overall performance in the environment."
  • Patrick William Sholl, Barrington Research: Asked about capital allocation post-reinvestment cycle. Paris answered, "The Hilton Milwaukee is by far the biggest project... And then we go into a more normal run rate for CapEx in the hotel business."
  • Sholl inquired about matinee pricing versus prior periods. Marcus explained, "It was sort of all over the place... We did that. I have to go back and look and see exactly where we are for all these."
  • Sholl also asked about new build and acquisition opportunities. Marcus said, "I don't think there's a lot of new build opportunities... we are looking at a few here and there... And then on the M&A side, again, it's so hard to predict what's going to happen there."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on forward-looking occupancy, pricing strategies, and capital allocation, showing a neutral to slightly positive tone as they sought granular details on segment performance and future plans.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, with Marcus stating, "We are thrilled to report second quarter results with significant growth," but was more measured and pragmatic during Q&A, often noting, "I don't have specific splits" or "it's very anecdotal."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed increased optimism given the improved performance, with management's tone shifting from defensive explanations of headwinds to emphasizing momentum and successful execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a marked improvement in revenue growth, adjusted EBITDA, and net earnings compared to Q1, when challenges in the film slate and ongoing hotel renovations were more acute.
  • Guidance language evolved to signal a "meaningful step down in capital expenditures" after 2025, compared to last quarter's expectation of elevated spending due to ongoing renovations.
  • Analysts in both quarters probed for more detailed breakdowns by geography and segment, but in Q2, they increasingly asked about the impact and sustainability of new pricing strategies in theaters and the timing of CapEx normalization.
  • Management confidence increased compared to the more cautious tone in Q1, with a stronger focus on the company's resilience and post-renovation growth opportunities.
  • The strategic focus shifted from managing through disruptions to capitalizing on completed projects and growing group business and attendance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged headwinds in hotel RevPAR due to the Hilton Milwaukee renovation, with Paris stating, "Our occupancy rate decrease was impacted by the Hilton Milwaukee renovation, while guest rooms were out of service."
  • Marcus noted increased uncertainty in the hotel business: "While we've not yet seen any meaningful change in transient demand or significant cancellations of group business... there remains an increased level of incremental uncertainty compared to where we were a year ago."
  • The company also cited underperformance in theater box office relative to national trends due to pricing strategies and Midwestern market challenges.
  • Analysts raised concerns about CapEx timing, the sustainability of pricing strategies, and the impact of renovations, all of which management addressed with operational updates and forward-looking adjustments.

Final Takeaway

Second quarter results for The Marcus Corporation reflected a successful rebound in both theater attendance and group hotel bookings, driven by a stronger film slate and the completion of major renovation milestones. Management outlined a plan for a significant reduction in capital expenditures after 2025, emphasizing readiness to capitalize on growth opportunities while maintaining flexibility for shareholder returns. The leadership team remains confident in its strategy of balancing attendance growth with pricing optimization and expects continued strength in both core business segments as renovation impacts subside and new investments begin to pay off.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Blackstone to reopen NYC headquarters Aug. 4 - report
data.description:
Blackstone (NYSE:BX) is set to reopen on Monday its New York City headquarters after the alternative asset manager shut the office down after a deadly shooting, according to a media report published Friday.

The company's 345 Park Ave headquarters will open Aug. 4 for any staff who wish to return, Bloomberg reported, citing an internal memo.

Employees were also given the choice to work from home all week.

Normal scheduling at the office is expected to resume Aug. 11, Blackstone (NYSE:BX) told staff, Bloomberg reported, with accommodations for employees uneasy about returning by that date.

data.title: Arbor Realty Trust outlines pathway to $4B agency origination with new securitization platform and heightened REO guidance amid competitive market
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Arbor Realty Trust (ABR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Ivan Paul Kaufman, President and CEO, reported a highly active first half of the year with significant improvements in the company’s balance sheet and progress in addressing delinquencies and REO assets despite the challenging environment. Kaufman stated that the company completed its first high-yield unsecured debt offering, raising $500 million, which was used to pay off all convertible debt and add $200 million of additional liquidity. Kaufman explained, “This is a tremendous accomplishment, especially in this environment...we received a BB rating on our corporate credit from both Moody's and Fitch, reinforcing the quality of our platform and the value of our diversified business model.”

  • Kaufman highlighted the issuance of the industry’s first build-to-rent securitization totaling $800 million, describing it as a “landmark transaction” that paves the way for a securitization platform in the single-family rental business. He emphasized the potential to “scale up this business and gain market share” due to increased efficiencies and a takeout to the CLO market.

  • Kaufman indicated that 2025 is considered a transitional year, with elevated rates continuing to impact agency originations and asset transitions. He noted, “We took back approximately $188 million of REO assets. $115 million of which we were able to flip to new sponsors and assume our debt. This brings our REO book to approximately $300 million as of June 30.” Kaufman updated REO guidance to $400 million to $600 million, citing recent trends.

  • Paul Anthony Elenio, Chief Financial Officer, commented, “In the second quarter, we produced distributable earnings of $52.1 million or $0.25 per share and $62.5 million or $0.30 a share, excluding $10.5 million of onetime realized losses from the sale of 2 REO assets in the second quarter.”

Outlook

  • Kaufman reiterated agency origination guidance for 2025 of $3.5 billion to $4 billion, citing strong July performance and a robust pipeline that could result in “originating approximately $2 billion in the third quarter, which would be one of the single largest production quarters in our history.”

  • Bridge loan production guidance for the year remains at $1.5 billion to $2 billion, with Kaufman noting, “whether we come in on the low end or the high end of the range will be dependent upon the market conditions and the interest rate environment.”

  • Construction lending guidance of $250 million to $500 million is expected to be surpassed, with $265 million closed in the first six months and $144 million in July. Kaufman said, “we feel we will easily beat the guidance we gave.”

  • Management described 2025 as a “transitional year” and expects challenges to persist due to REO drag and the current interest rate environment, with opportunities to grow earnings and dividends again in 2026 if rate relief materializes.

Financial Results

  • Elenio reported distributable earnings of $52.1 million or $0.25 per share, and $62.5 million or $0.30 per share excluding one-time realized losses. Quarterly ROE was 10%.

  • Total delinquencies declined to $529 million at June 30 (from $654 million at March 31). The 60-plus day delinquent loans decreased to $472 million from $511 million last quarter. Loans less than 60 days past due dropped to $57 million from $143 million.

  • Agency originations reached $857 million, with $807 million in loan sales and strong margins of 1.69%. Mortgage servicing rights income was $10.9 million related to $853 million of committed loans.

  • The investment portfolio in balance sheet lending grew to $11.6 billion, with an all-in yield of 7.86%. The average yield on core assets declined to 7.95% from 8.15% the previous quarter. Net interest spread narrowed to 1.08% from 1.26%.

  • The leverage ratio improved to 3:1 from a peak of 4:1 nearly three years ago.

Q&A

  • Steven Cole Delaney, Citizens JMP Securities: Asked about the drop in net interest income and unusual items. Paul Anthony Elenio explained, “We had a few more delinquencies in the second quarter...we reversed $5 million of paying accrual and $3 million of that were on loans that we foreclosed on.”

  • Delaney questioned the potential peak of REO assets. Kaufman responded that 2025 is viewed as a transitional year with efforts to “accelerate this process as much as we can and get all of this behind us as much as possible,” adjusting REO guidance upward.

  • Jade Joseph Rahmani, KBW: Inquired about increased interest from outside parties in the REO and sub-performing loan book. Kaufman confirmed, “There is a tremendous amount of dollars chasing distressed deals...when we do have a deal that's in the distress, we have multiple bidders.”

  • Richard Barry Shane, JPMorgan: Asked about realized losses on REO and PIK interest accrual. Elenio detailed that $95 million of PIK was on the balance sheet at June 30, with $15 million related to mezz and PE and $80 million in bridge lending, accruing about $15 million a quarter.

  • Crispin Elliot Love, Piper Sandler: Asked about SFR strategy. Kaufman said the company intends to “put a big effort into to continue to build our market share in that space” following the successful CLO securitization.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts exhibited a neutral to slightly positive tone, probing for color on REO trends, delinquency resolution, and strategy for SFR and agency originations, with no overt skepticism or negative sentiment.

  • Management maintained a confident but cautious tone, frequently referencing the “challenging environment” while underscoring achievements in capital markets, securitizations, and pipeline growth. Phrases like “we are confident” and “tremendous accomplishment” signaled optimism, balanced with transparency about ongoing headwinds.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management tone remained steady, with the management's confidence buttressed by new securitization and capital market milestones.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for agency and bridge loan originations remained consistent, but REO guidance increased from $400-500 million to $400-600 million, reflecting more aggressive asset repositioning.

  • The company completed its first high-yield unsecured debt offering and an industry-first build-to-rent securitization, both new developments versus the prior quarter.

  • Delinquencies declined materially quarter-over-quarter, and distributable earnings per share adjusted for one-time items dropped slightly.

  • Management reaffirmed 2025 as a transitional year, but highlighted increased pipeline strength in agency loans and SFR expansion.

  • Analyst focus continued to center on liquidity, asset quality, and the impact of market volatility, while management’s confidence was more pronounced due to capital market successes and a robust loan pipeline.

Risks and Concerns

  • Elevated rates continue to pressure agency originations and borrower transitions to fixed-rate loans, generating short-term earnings drag.

  • Rising REO asset balances, now guided to $400-600 million, indicate ongoing asset resolution challenges.

  • Delinquencies remain a concern, though progress in resolution is evident. Management anticipates further new delinquencies if the current rate environment persists.

  • The process of foreclosing and repositioning REO assets is expected to be a temporary headwind to earnings until assets generate current income or are disposed of.

  • The competitive lending landscape is prompting selectivity, with management unwilling to compromise on credit or structure to win deals.

Final Takeaway

Arbor Realty Trust delivered a quarter of transformational capital market transactions and operational progress, highlighted by the industry’s first build-to-rent securitization and a $500 million unsecured debt raise. Guidance for origination growth remains intact, and the company anticipates record agency volumes in the coming quarter. While the environment remains challenging with persistent REO and delinquency pressures, management’s actions to bolster liquidity, diversify funding, and scale high-return business lines position the platform for renewed growth as market conditions stabilize in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cousins Properties raises 2025 FFO guidance to $2.82 per share amid strong Sun Belt leasing and Dallas expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cousins Properties (CUZ) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Michael Colin Connolly, President and CEO, opened the call acknowledging the passing of Tom Cousins and emphasizing the company’s commitment to his legacy. He reported, “We had a strong second quarter at Cousins. On the earnings front, the team delivered $0.70 a share in FFO, which was $0.01 above consensus. Same-property net operating income increased 1.2% on a cash basis and 1.6% year-to-date. Leasing remained very strong. We completed 334,000 square feet of leases during the quarter and remarkably, 80% of that was new or expansion leases.”
  • Connolly announced the acquisition of The Link in Uptown Dallas, stating, “We purchased The Link, a trophy lifestyle office property in Uptown Dallas, which grows our presence in a strategic market.” He highlighted that the acquisition would be funded through proceeds from an unsecured note offering, settlement of previously issued common shares, and potential asset dispositions.
  • Connolly added, “Given the solid second quarter performance, we have increased the midpoint of our guidance to $2.82 a share, which represents 4.8% growth rate over last year. I believe there's a perception that an office company cannot grow earnings. We are proving that wrong and for the second consecutive year, and we are excited to do it.”
  • Jane Kennedy Hicks, Chief Investment Officer, stated, “Earlier this week, we closed on The Link in Uptown Dallas, a trophy asset that fits squarely into our lifestyle Sun Belt office strategy. We acquired the property for $218 million or $747 per square foot, pricing that represents the discount to replacement cost and is immediately accretive to earnings.”
  • Gregg D. Adzema, CFO, commented, “Overall, as Colin stated upfront, our second quarter results were outstanding. Second-generation cash leasing spreads were positive for the 45th straight quarter, same property year-over-year cash only increased and leasing velocity was strong.”

Outlook

  • Management raised full-year 2025 FFO guidance to a range of $2.79 to $2.85 per share, with a new midpoint of $2.82 per share, up $0.03 from last quarter and representing 4.8% growth over 2024. Connolly attributed the increase to “accretion from our acquisition of The Link, higher parking income and better-than-forecast execution on the unsecured note that we issued in June.”
  • Management reiterated the assumption of “no SOFR cuts in 2025.”
  • Guidance continues to factor in the future settlement of approximately $2.3 million of previously issued shares, with the possibility of using proceeds from potential asset sales instead.

Financial Results

  • The company reported $0.70 per share in FFO for the quarter. Same-property cash NOI grew 1.2% in Q2 2025 and 1.6% year-to-date. GAAP NOI increased 3.2% during the quarter compared to last year.
  • Richard Hickson, EVP of Operations, noted portfolio occupancy of 91.6% leased and 89.1% weighted average occupancy. Both figures were down from last quarter due to the known OneTrust move-out in Atlanta.
  • The company completed 41 office leases totaling 334,000 square feet with a weighted average lease term of 7.9 years. 80% of this leasing volume was new or expansion leases.
  • The Link was acquired for $218 million and is 94% leased, with a weighted average remaining lease term of 9.3 years. The anticipated initial cash yield over the next 12 months is 6.7% with a GAAP yield of 8.3%.
  • The company completed a $500 million investment-grade bond offering during the quarter at an initial yield of 5.25%.

Q&A

  • Anthony Paolone, JPMorgan, asked about underwriting The Link’s 6.7% yield and acquisition rationale. Connolly responded, “We were excited. And I think anytime we can identify a compelling investment opportunity with that type of profile that continues to upgrade the quality of our portfolio... below replacement cost is -- checks all the boxes for us.”
  • Jana Galan, Bank of America, inquired about lease roll-ups and noncore dispositions. Hickson stated Phoenix was the only market without roll-ups this quarter, attributing it to a single tough lease comp. Connolly explained dispositions are “driven by new investment opportunities that we identify” and will prioritize older vintage or higher CapEx properties.
  • Stephen Sakwa, Evercore ISI, questioned Neuhoff’s mixed leasing momentum. Hicks responded, “We remain really excited about Neuhoff... apartments continue to lease up at a really nice pace... On the office side, we did see a little bit of a lull earlier in the spring, but we've seen the pickup in tours and requests for proposals.”
  • John Kim, BMO, asked about mark-to-market opportunities at The Link. Hicks confirmed “about a $20 spread between in-place rents and where we think trends can be achieved today.”
  • Blaine Heck, Wells Fargo, asked about leasing pipeline trends. Hickson said, “Financial services is the heaviest contributor to our pipeline today... legal was pretty heavy kind of earlier in the year... tech still is playing a more modest but still meaningful role.”
  • Several analysts sought updates on capital recycling, new market entry, and the impact of major move-outs, with management reiterating focus on disciplined acquisitions and active portfolio management.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was generally positive but included pointed questions on leasing trends, capital allocation, and market risks, seeking clarity on specific market dynamics and the sustainability of leasing momentum.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone throughout, especially during prepared remarks. Connolly stated, “We are proving that wrong and for the second consecutive year, and we are excited to do it.”
  • Compared to last quarter, both analysts and management displayed strengthened confidence, with management more assertive on earnings growth and portfolio strategy.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance midpoint increased from $2.79 to $2.82 per share, reflecting higher growth expectations than last quarter’s 3.7% rise.
  • Leasing volume decreased sequentially (334,000 vs. 539,000 square feet), but the proportion of new or expansion leases rose significantly.
  • Portfolio occupancy declined slightly due to known move-outs, but management reiterated expectations for occupancy to build back later in the year.
  • Strategic focus shifted further toward Sun Belt expansion and portfolio upgrading, highlighted by the Uptown Dallas acquisition.
  • Management’s tone grew more confident on market tightening and capital market access, while analysts’ focus remained on sustainability of growth and execution of strategic initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing uncertainties around tariffs and interest rates.
  • Anticipated further occupancy decline through Q3, with recovery projected later in the year.
  • Large expirations, such as Bank of America in Charlotte, were addressed as anticipated and not expected to alter overall trends.
  • Analyst concerns centered on market-specific leasing softness, competitive acquisition dynamics, and the execution of capital recycling.

Final Takeaway

Cousins Properties highlighted a strong Q2 2025, raising its full-year FFO guidance and executing a high-profile acquisition in Dallas. Management emphasized robust leasing demand, successful portfolio upgrades, and confidence in Sun Belt office market fundamentals. The company’s proactive capital markets activity and disciplined asset recycling support its outlook for continued earnings growth and strategic expansion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: DigitalBridge evaluating sale of Vantage Data stake - Bloomberg
data.description:
DigitalBrige (NYSE:DBRG) is evaluating selling a stake in the Asia Pacific business of Vantage Data Centers for as much as $1 billion. 

DigitalBridge (NYSE:DBRG) has been in discussions for a stake sale, which could raise $500 million to $1 billion, according to a Bloomberg report on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter. Pension funds and infrastructure funds may be interested in Vantage. 

Deliberations are ongoing, and there's no guarantee that any deal will come to fruition, according to the report. 

DigitalBridge declined to comment to Bloomberg.
